skill.name: upgrading-expo
skill.description: Guides agents through Expo SDK upgrades, dependency fixes, cache cleanup, and migration checks for React Native apps.
skill.summary: Upgrade Expo SDK projects with focused dependency, cache, and migration guidance.
skill.author: expo

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many Markdown backticks, documentation URLs, and API names as dangerous code patterns. Review found no executable skill scripts, credential access, prompt injection, or malicious intent, but the skill does instruct agents to run project-changing Expo, CocoaPods, Gradle, and deletion commands. Publish with a warning that commands should be reviewed before execution in user projects.

medium_findings.title: Project-Changing Shell Commands in Upgrade Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends shell commands that install packages, clear caches, regenerate native directories, and remove local dependency folders. These are normal Expo upgrade actions, but an agent should not run them without user awareness because they can modify project state and remove generated files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly present in Markdown guidance and include installation, cleanup, and prebuild actions. They appear legitimate for Expo upgrades, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Static Command-Execution Alerts Are Mostly Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: Most external command alerts refer to Markdown code fences, inline package names, JSX snippets, or migration examples. I found no executable script file, dynamic command construction, or hidden code path in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is visible Markdown documentation, and no executable source file is present among the scanned files. Some shell commands remain operationally sensitive, which is covered by the medium finding.

low_findings.title: Network and Weak-Crypto Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Expo documentation and migration references. The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts appear to be triggered by ordinary words in documentation, with no cryptographic implementation or hash function usage found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain documentation URLs or migration prose rather than network code or cryptographic operations. There is no evidence of data transfer, hashing, encryption, or credential handling.

low_findings.title: Browser Storage and Reconnaissance Alerts Lack Malicious Context
low_findings.description: The browser storage hit is a deprecated package table entry for AsyncStorage replacement guidance. The reconnaissance hits map to normal mobile build cleanup instructions and section headings, with no host probing or network scanning behavior found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines are documentation about package migration and build cleanup. I found no semantic evidence of credential collection, browser storage access, system enumeration, or network reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands Require User Review Before Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains commands such as Expo installs, cache clearing, prebuild regeneration, CocoaPods update, and Gradle cleanup. These commands are appropriate for the stated task but should be treated as user-approved maintenance actions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and contextually related to Expo maintenance. The concern is safe execution practice, not confirmed malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Expo SDK upgrades can break dependencies, native builds, and React patterns. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured checklist for safer upgrades.
actual_capabilities: Guides Expo and dependency upgrades with expo install commands.
Identifies cache cleanup steps for Expo, iOS, Android, and bare workflow projects.
Summarizes New Architecture migration checks for Expo SDK 53 and later.
Explains React 19 migration changes for context providers, refs, and hooks.
Documents React Compiler setup and cleanup for Expo SDK 54 and later.
Lists deprecated Expo packages and common replacements.
limitations: Does not inspect package release notes beyond the included guidance.
Does not guarantee that every third-party native module supports the target SDK.
May recommend shell commands that change project files and dependencies.
Does not replace full testing on iOS, Android, and web targets.

use_cases.title: Upgrade a Production Expo App
use_cases.description: Plan the SDK upgrade, dependency repair, cache cleanup, and regression checks before release.

use_cases.title: Fix Broken Native Builds
use_cases.description: Use focused iOS, Android, and prebuild cleanup steps after an Expo version change.

use_cases.title: Modernize React Patterns
use_cases.description: Apply React 19 and React Compiler guidance while upgrading to recent Expo SDK versions.

prompt_templates.title: Plan My Upgrade
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to upgrade my Expo app. Review my current SDK version, dependencies, and workflow, then give me a safe step-by-step plan.

prompt_templates.title: Repair Dependencies
prompt_templates.prompt: My Expo upgrade has dependency warnings and build errors. Identify likely fixes, explain each command before running it, and list tests to run after changes.

prompt_templates.title: Check Native Migration Risk
prompt_templates.scenario: New Architecture and native module review
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this Expo project for New Architecture migration risk. Check native modules, Reanimated requirements, prebuild needs, and development build impact.

prompt_templates.title: Modernize After SDK 54
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced React 19 and React Compiler migration
prompt_templates.prompt: After upgrading to Expo SDK 54, review the React codebase for React 19 updates and React Compiler cleanup opportunities. Prioritize safe, testable changes.

output_examples.input: Upgrade this Expo project from SDK 52 to SDK 54.
output: Checks the current Expo, React Native, and package versions.
Proposes dependency updates and cache cleanup steps.
Flags New Architecture and React 19 migration areas.
Lists validation steps for iOS, Android, and web.

output_examples.input: The iOS build fails after upgrading Expo.
output: Reviews whether native directories were regenerated correctly.
Suggests CocoaPods and Expo cache cleanup steps.
Calls out commands that require approval before execution.
Recommends focused tests for native modules.

output_examples.input: Enable React Compiler in my Expo app.
output: Confirms SDK and New Architecture requirements.
Identifies manual memoization that may be removed later.
Keeps behavioral changes separate from compiler setup.

best_practices: Review every shell command before execution in a project repository.
Commit or back up the project before cache cleanup or prebuild regeneration.
Run platform-specific tests after dependency, native, or React migration changes.
anti_patterns: Running cleanup commands before understanding the current Expo workflow.
Treating dependency installation as complete without testing native features.
Mixing React 19 refactors with unrelated app changes in one review.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides upgrade guidance and command examples. Agents should ask before running project-changing commands.

faq.question: Which Expo versions does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on SDK 53 and SDK 54 topics, including New Architecture, React 19, and React Compiler.

faq.question: Can it fix native module compatibility issues?
faq.answer: It can guide investigation and cleanup, but third-party module compatibility must be verified with module documentation and testing.

faq.question: Is this useful for bare workflow apps?
faq.answer: Yes, but bare workflow commands need extra review because they can affect iOS and Android project files.

faq.question: Does it replace Expo release notes?
faq.answer: No. It gives a practical checklist and points users to release notes for version-specific changes.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
